- ForceUpdateModal component is implemented with version/minVersion check from AppController.php
- Two version values must be synced: app.json 'version' field and AppController.php 'minVersion' field
- AppController.php has 'force' flag to control whether update modal is blocking or dismissible
- Release workflow: update app.json version → build APK → npm run build:release → update AppController.php minVersion → restart backend
- ForceUpdateModal shows 'Update Available' with Skip button when force=false, or 'Update Required' without skip when force=true
- APK contains built app that checks /api/app-version on each login
- Server-side minVersion in AppController.php triggers ForceUpdateModal for users with older APK
- APK does not need to be rebuilt for server-side version updates
- Release workflow: build APK locally, then update minVersion on server in AppController.php
- force flag in AppController.php controls if update modal blocks user (true) or can be dismissed (false)
- When force=false, users see 'Update Available' with Skip button; when force=true, see 'Update Required' without skip option
- The APK update mechanism is split: server-side minVersion (AppController.php) controls the modal, client-side ForceUpdateModal logic is baked into the APK
- Changing minVersion on server does NOT require rebuilding the frontend APK
- Users with old APK (before ForceUpdateModal was implemented) will NOT get the update modal even if minVersion is increased - they lack the check logic in their APK
- Users need to install the new APK once to receive future update notifications controlled by server-side minVersion
- After new APK is distributed to all users, future releases only require updating minVersion on server and copying APK to public/apk
- The update modal shows a 'Download & Install Update' button that lets users download and install the new APK from public/apk/ without manual reinstallation

- Root cause of login crash was react-native-css-interop@0.2.3's JSX wrapper calling require('./components') on every JSX render, causing SafeAreaProvider to resolve to undefined

active_projects.name: Claudish Proxy Shared Brain
active_projects.summary: Make Claude Desktop third-party mode feel like a persistent cross-device assistant with reliable web access and shared memory.

Claude Desktop.summary: Third-party mode is active; native web and Chrome capabilities can be inconsistent, so the proxy should preserve client-compatible tool names and resolve web fetches locally when needed.

MiniMax M2.7.summary: Used as the upstream model behind Claude-compatible requests through the proxy.

recent_events.summary: Investigated Claude Desktop third-party mode and found native web/chrome capability restrictions were separate from the proxy's own fetch implementation.
Added Claude-compatible web tool aliases and began routing workspace-style web fetch/search names through local proxy execution.
